food_subgroup: Braised chicken in mild yogurt-almond-cream sauce

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific concept for Chicken Korma. Nearest parent is a mild braised chicken curry with dairy. A new concept for Mughlai-style korma preparations is recommended.
ontology.foodex2_description: Braised chicken in mild dairy-nut sauce, Mughlai style (closest parent)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code exists for Chicken Korma; A03SM used as closest parent for braised poultry in dairy-based sauce.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept found for Chicken Korma or Mughlai braised chicken preparations.

allergens: may contain traces of gluten depending on preparation

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Chicken Korma is approximately 250 g, equivalent to one bowl of curry with several chicken pieces, as typically served at a North Indian or Mughlai restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken Korma — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods, Entry F11020017; cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN); Health Promotion Board (HPB), Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods for Chicken Korma (entry F11020017), cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Food Composition Database. Copper, manganese, and vitamin K values are estimated from enriched composition data with confidence 0.9; all other macros and micronutrients are verified at confidence 1.0.

gi_source.title: Estimated GI for Chicken Korma based on analogous low-carbohydrate braised chicken dish with dairy and nut sauce; reference: University of Sydney GI Database — mixed meat and dairy dishes
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Chicken Korma in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 35 is estimated based on analogous braised chicken dishes with high fat (cream, nuts) and moderate protein content, which substantially attenuate glucose release from the minimal carbohydrate (5 g/100 g) present. Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (35 × 5.0 × 2.5) / 100 = 4.375, rounded to 4.4 for the full serving; per-serving GL reported as 4.4. Note: GL per 100 g = (35 × 5.0) / 100 = 1.75.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content 318 mg/100 g; a 250 g serving delivers approximately 795 mg sodium, representing about 35% of the 2,300 mg daily limit.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals managing hypertension should limit Chicken Korma to occasional consumption and request reduced-salt preparation when ordering at restaurants, as a single 250 g bowl can contribute up to 795 mg sodium.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content (5 g/100 g) and estimated low GI of 35; high protein (14.5 g) and fat (14.2 g) further blunt postprandial glucose response.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Chicken Korma is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes in moderate portions, as its low carbohydrate load and high protein-fat content produce a minimal glycemic response; a 250 g serving yields a glycemic load of approximately 4.4.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat content of 5.5 g/100 g (13.75 g per 250 g serving) from cream, ghee, and nuts; cholesterol 62 mg/100 g.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume Chicken Korma in moderation due to its elevated saturated fat content (5.5 g/100 g), which may raise LDL cholesterol with frequent intake; limiting portion size to 150 g and choosing preparations with reduced cream is advisable.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High saturated fat from cream (5.5 g/100 g) may promote systemic inflammation if consumed frequently; however, cardamom and cinnamon have modest anti-inflammatory properties.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis may consume Chicken Korma occasionally, but should be mindful that frequent intake of high-saturated-fat preparations may exacerbate inflammatory markers.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chicken Korma is one of India's most celebrated Mughlai curries, originating from the royal kitchens of the Mughal Empire in North India. The dish consists of chicken pieces braised slowly in a rich, mildly spiced, pale-golden sauce made from whisked yogurt, fried onion paste (birista), finely ground cashew or almond paste, heavy cream, and aromatic whole spices including green cardamom, cloves, cinnamon, and bay leaf. The cooking technique involves low-heat braising to prevent yogurt from curdling, resulting in a velvety, silky gravy. The flavour profile is distinctly mild in chilli heat — aromatic, slightly sweet, and deeply rich with nut and dairy fat. It is often finished with saffron strands or kewra water for a floral note. Nutritionally, Chicken Korma provides 192 kcal per 100 g, with 14.5 g protein, 14.2 g fat (5.5 g saturated), and only 5 g carbohydrates, making it a low-carbohydrate, high-protein main dish. Its estimated glycemic index of 35 (low) and glycemic load of approximately 4.4 per 250 g serving make it suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. Sodium content of 318 mg/100 g warrants moderation for hypertensive individuals. It is a popular choice at Indian restaurant buffets in Singapore and across South and Southeast Asia, and is widely considered the mildest of the classic Indian chicken curries.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrients and most micronutrients sourced from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (entry F11020017), a primary approved regional source for Indian dishes, cross-referenced with HPB Singapore. Copper, manganese, and vitamin K are estimated from enriched composition data at confidence 0.9. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database methodology. Overall record confidence is high.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for Chicken Korma not available in ICMR-NIN or HPB Singapore databases as of 2026-04-13. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported in the primary ICMR-NIN source entry for this preparation. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero per ICMR-NIN source). Copper (0.15 mg), manganese (0.2 mg), and vitamin K (2.5 mcg) values are estimated from enriched composition data with confidence 0.9, as these micronutrients are not individually reported in the primary ICMR-NIN entry. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Chicken Korma exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (35 × 5.0 × 2.5) / 100 = 4.375 ≈ 4.4. Duplicate portion entries in the input (four identical '1 serving (250g)' records) have been consolidated to a single canonical serving of 250 g. The hypertension health suitability note in the input incorrectly marked this as 'suitable: true' without adequate caution; this has been revised to concern_level 'moderate' to reflect the 795 mg sodium per serving. FoodEx2 code A03SM is used as closest parent; no exact FoodEx2 code exists for Mughlai-style braised chicken korma.
